comments.title: Clue Stick Gratefully Accepted
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Well, I've only been practicing Geomancy for a week now and I've already gone and gotten myself into trouble. I dutifully performed a daily reading every day except Saturday, when Typhoon Hagibis swept past us. We were out of the direct path, thankfully, but we did batten the hatches, and I heeded the tradition not to divine when weather is rough.

Anyway. I'd like your help talking me down off of a ledge here, divinationally speaking at least. I understand that beginners often see dire portents for everyday humdrum events. Even knowing this and recognizing that this is what's likely happening, I still am experiencing the same right now. My hope is that you will now just bop me with a clue stick, and I can go forth with a better idea of how not to see tragedy every reading I do.

What's happened is that, being a little short of funds lately, I thought I would try out the "special question" regarding obtaining money. So I asked, simply, "What is the best way for me to obtain money?" The four Mothers were Fortuna Minor, Fortuna Minor, Albus, and Fortuna Major. So, Houses 1 and 2 are the same figure, and it's positive-- a great sign! Free money, coming in a surprising way, and I don't even have to work for it. Moreover, the Judge was Fortuna Major. What's there not to like?  

Calculating the Index gave me a source of unexpected resources (and Fortuna Major) in the 4th House. Again, very positive. As I don't own any real estate, I thought perhaps it had to do with my father. Money from my family? Hmmm. Next, the Part of Fortune-- where to look for good money luck-- gave me 8th house. Death or occultism. "May it not be money coming from a death", I thought... then I saw the figure in that house was Tristitia. Crap. Well, that got my attention. Now a little nervous, I decided it was time to take a closer look at the rest of the chart to see what the figures seemed to be saying.

Fortuna Minor passes through the 1st, 2nd, 5th, and 6th houses. As I am hoping to shift my income to earning more from my musical ventures, 5th and 6th houses could be a positive sign in that direction. On the other hand, perhaps it comes from money being given to my daughter (5th house) from a lawyer (6th house, possibly)? Albus is in the 3rd and 12th houses-- in other words, bracketing both querent and quesited. Tough wisdom (Albus) of regarding limits (12th) taught to me, my brother and/or my father's brothers (3rd)? Albus is also the Left Witness, indicating that my future state regarding my enquiry about money sources is also some kind of wisdom gained. The Judge, Fortuna Major, also appears in the 4th house (my dad? an ending?) and 7th (intense human relationships, agreements-- such as a will?). The Right Witness, which I originally took to mean my sadness as the Querent at having very little money, took on a new meaning when I find it in the 8th house (death, ghosts), and the 10th house (my mother?). (I'm guessing the 8th house is a common source of "dire warnings" confusion for beginners... but the tragic reading seems to fit too well for me to avoid that trap at the moment.) Finally, since there were only two houses I hadn't looked at, I saw Populus in the 9th (long journeys) and 11th (friends, promises, sources of help)-- an abundance of long journeys and people giving help. (Like an overseas trip to a funeral?) The Reconciler/Result of the Result gave Via-- a (big) change. And following the Cardines told the the story Fortuna Minor, Tristitia, Fortuna Major, Fortuna Major: an initially positive-seeming development leads first to sadness, which leads to big money... and an aftermath of big money. 

So... 

Perhaps followup readings might clarify the situation and assuage my worries. However, I have some questions I'd like to ask first.

(1) At least at a quick glance, does my reading of "father dies and leaves me money" seem a reasonable interpretation here? Would a less overwrought scenario suggest itself to you, given these figures?

(2) I did not consecrate any divination tools, invoke any geomantic spirits, or pray in advance of my reading, nor do I do any kind of regular banishing ritual at present. Is it possible that this could have made any difference? For example, it occurs to me that I didn't specify a time frame for my reading. Perhaps it is a "correct" reading but I wasn't connecting with a guiding intelligence that cared to take into account my implied relatively short time frame, and so in this hypothesis, a "naughty" or even just "dumb" spirit gave me a correct answer which won't come to pass for years yet-- thus causing unnecessary emotional havoc.

(3) Assuming that I was just seeing apocalypse where none exists. Are there markers that show up which would more clearly indicate the fact if it really *was* a marker of tragedy? Is the ability to judge that mostly a matter of intuition, and it comes with experience?

replies.title: Re: Clue Stick Gratefully Accepted
replies.content:
This is a very common experience for beginning occultists. I don't think I've ever met anyone who got into divination who didn't spend a while turning every reading into a portent of hideous doom. The 8th house in geomancy and astrology is one common focus of that habit. (Another is the last three cards in the suit of Swords in the Tarot.) It's especially common when people get fixated on subtle indicators rather than the basic rules of interpretation. That's what's going on here.

The first thing you do when you cast a geomantic reading is settle which of the two kinds of charts you're going to use -- shield or house. If it's a shield reading, the witnesses and judge give you the answer; if it's a house reading, the presence or absence of a mode of perfection gives you the answer. The special questions are ways of modifying the basic house chart reading, they don't stand alone. So the first thing to ask yourself in this reading is whether there's a mode of perfection connecting the house of the querent and the house of the quesited, and what that tells you.

In this chart, the house of the querent -- the 1st house -- has Fortuna Minor, and so does the house of the quesited -- the 2nd house. In a money reading, that tells me that you already have the money you need, or can get it quickly with the help of others (Fortuna Minor has this implication). The fact that it passes to the 5th and 6th houses tells me that you have a variety of options for getting more money if you want it, including investment (5th house) and employment (6th house). That's the heart of the reading. Trying to read all the houses will land you in the quandary you're in, trying to find meanings for a galaxy of figures that can mean all kinds of things.

So you asked a straightforward question and you got a straightforward answer. You can certainly get more money if you want it, either by investing or by getting a better job. The useful tip given by the Part of Fortune? Don't go into debt (the 8th house is the house of debt -- money owed to other people -- and Tristitia there is a bad sign).

It takes practice to learn how to focus on what matters in a geomantic reading and leave out all the things that don't matter. For now -- since you've been doing it for a week -- forget about the Index, the Part of Fortune, the cardines, the special questions, and the rest of it. You'll master geomancy far more effectively if, for the next three months, you interpret only the Witnesses and Judge, and leave it at that. Then spend three months factoring in the Way of the Points and the four Triplicities if those are appropriate. Then start work on the house chart, interpreting only the presence or absence of a mode of perfection, and stick with that until you've got that down cold; then, and only then, start adding in other factors -- and do it one at a time.

Oh, and a geomantic reading by and large won't tell you about a tragedy unless you ask it about one. The geomantic intelligences are very literal-minded, and will tell you what you ask them about, in a simple, straightforward, and precise manner.

replies.content:

I've been casting one daily for three weeks and trying to make a forecast for the day and then evaluate it at the end of the day.

In a daily forecast are there any modes of perfection? Anything to look out for or ignore?

Thanks,
Tim

replies.title: Re: Clue Stick Gratefully Accepted
replies.content: I recommend doing shield charts rather than house charts for daily geomantic readings. In that case, to begin with, the right witness is you, the left witness is the situation you encounter that day, and the judge is the outcome. Keep it simple until you can make sense of that, and then add in the four triplicities. Using the house chart for a daily reading should be left until you're really good at house charts; otherwise it's really easy to get lost among the intricacies of meaning and interpretation.

replies.title: Re: Clue Stick Gratefully Accepted
replies.content:
Thanks very much for this. Seriously, this whack with a clue stick was extremely gratefully accepted.

I think I was getting overconfident and overexcited with my "new toy", as the mechanical side of geomancy is still quite new and fun for me, and I was looking to try every flavor on the cart all at once, so to speak. I am somewhat amazed that I could literally recognize the trap that I was falling into there ahead of time, and still fall right in. Clearly geomancy is, like any worthwhile skill, something that "looks so easy" until you actually start trying it.

Thank you for the advice on how to progress. It seems it will take time to learn to calibrate my intuitions to find the sweet spot where I'm sensing the information that matters.

replies.title: Re: Clue Stick Gratefully Accepted
replies.content: You're most welcome. It's a very common issue, and yeah, it usually takes a direct encounter with the trap -- even if you know about it in the abstract -- to be able to recognize it and get out from it.

comments.title: Lazy Libra
comments.content: I  read somewhere that one of the negative personality traits of Libras is laziness. I immediately recognized myself in that, as I am a Libra who struggles with laziness. I was wondering if there was anything that you could recommend that I do to counteract this?

replies.title: Re: Lazy Libra
replies.content:
Sure. To begin with, never try to change a habit until you understand the reasons for the habit. Habits are repeated choices. What does laziness do for you? How do you benefit from it? How does it protect you, or help you deal with certain people in your family of origin? Do some serious journaling until you can regard your laziness impartially, without getting down on yourself, as a habit you took up for a reason, and one that you will now start to let go of for another reason.

Then make little changes. Never, but never, try to change your habits all at once; that's a very common way people make sure that they fail. Each day choose one small thing to do that's active, energetic, and enthusiastic rather than lazy. Just one thing, remember! Run the errand, write the email, do that one thing, and then sit back and let yourself be lazy for the rest of the day, to the extent that you can. Only when that one thing happens automatically, and you find yourself doing other things without making a big deal of it, should you go further.

Also, consider using an affirmation. The journaling will among other things show you what emotional needs your laziness is meeting. Choose an affirmation to help you fill those emotional needs in other ways. You can find instructions for that here.

replies.title: Re: Lazy Libra
replies.content:
Not the op but wanted to say thank you for the sound advice to never just change habits without realizing why they're there. Luckily, I got this advice some years ago and I am very grateful for this.

It teaches you compassion and patience - not only with yourself, but also with others. If others behave annoying towards you, especially people who are close to you, I found it very valuable to ask myself what purpose this behavior might be needed for in that persons life. In some cases just thinking deeply about it may solve the whole issue.

So, thanks again!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Lazy Libra
replies.content: You're most welcome. It's good advice, and I was glad to get it.

replies.title: Re: Lazy Libra
replies.content:
A follow up question if I may (not the original commenter). In the DMH, in the Transforming Yourself section of the SOP, the example you wrote where you invoke and banish qualities in each of the four elements has laziness as being the quality banished in Earth. But there are several other qualities being banished in the other elements (wandering thoughts for Air, depressive moods for Water, etc).

Obviously that's just an example, but here are 4 habits being changed, which goes against the advice you've given above to never change more than one habit at once. Is that just because this banishing is done as part of a ritual working such as the SOP and thus has more powerful effects?

replies.title: Re: Lazy Libra
replies.content: Different practices have different requirements, and if you're doing an elemental balancing ritual, that's going to involve different requirements than doing a specific practice to change a single habit that concerns you.

comments.title: hu gadarn; political magic
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I am going to be a bit greedy and post a double question here:

First, where can I find more information about Hu Gadarn? He figures highly in your Celtic Golden Dawn pantheon, but there is not nearly so much information about him as there is about Lugh or Cernunnos. Is there a particular one of your books that would be a good source?

Ok here is the other question: I wonder what you think of this news story (excerpted) and a comment on it that I will explain below:

https://news.yahoo.com/aria-cost-marie-antoinette-her-head-sung-again-024705964.html

"The last time the strains of the aria “Oh Richard! Oh my king!” echoed around the Opera Royal at Versailles it was for Louis XVI and Marie-Antoinette just before they were dragged off to Paris and their doom by their revolting subjects. The piece from “Richard the Lionheart” by Andre Gretry, one of the tragic queen’s favourite composers, was last performed by the Palace of Versailles’ own opera company in 1789, as the ancien regime crumbled. This week it returned to the gilded chocolate-box opera, commissioned by the Sun King Louis XIV, after a 230-year break. The lavishly decorated theatre was used less than 20 times — most famously for the festivities that followed Marie-Antoinette’s marriage when she was just 14 — before she and her husband lost their heads in the French Revolution.
And it was the singing of the aria by the king’s bodyguards when the royal couple appeared at a banquet at the opera on October 1, 1789 that lead to their downfall, Versailles’ theatre and events director Laurent Brunner told AFP. Such a defiant display of royalist sentiment three months after the fall of Bastille incensed the hungry people of Paris, whose women marched out a few days later to take the royals back to the capital. “It caused a scandal in revolutionary Paris,” said Brunner, with radical leaders like Marat and Danton branding the banquet “counter-revolutionary”. Rumours circulated “that the revolutionary tricolour rosette has been trampled underfoot” by the royalist revellers, he added. [..] “Three days after (the news broke), the women of Paris marched on Versailles and the day after the palace was empty,” Brunner added."

This story was reported at Raul Ilargi Meijer's Automatic Earth site https://www.theautomaticearth.com/the-automatic-earth/

Raul is a peak oil writer who now does a daily news aggregation that has become my default daily newspaper. He has a regular commenter called Dr. D. who seems very astute (in every way except for being a climate change denier). Anyway, Dr. D. made the comment below about magic and the Marie Antoinette story, and I wonder if you have any thoughts, insights or additional knowledge about this that you could share with us? So much as they are erasing Britain, they are erasing France in a different way. For the hate of ego knows no bounds."

comments.title: Do chants evoke magical entities?
comments.content:
Amazingly-productive Archdruid, I frequent Magic Mondays and have learned a lot from the discussions, though it’s not really a path I follow. A beginner-style question I’m curious about: if a group engages in a practice that LOOKS like a magical incantation, but does not invoke any particular spirit or deity, do any of said entities listen and perhaps respond? I’m curious about this specifically in the case of the so-called “Anthem” of the group Extinction Rebellion. There is a ~1-minute YouTube video of the “anthem” being performed here, for those who are curious: https://youtu.be/7u7q3K052sA

Brief description of the video: eight people (seven apparently female and one male) march counterclockwise in a circle waving multi-colored flags bearing the XR logo and chanting:
“People gonna rise like the water
Gonna shut this system down.
In the voice of my great grand-daughter
Saying Climate Justice Now.”

So my specific questions: 1) Do spiritual entities listen to such things, and if so which entities? And 2) If any of these entities actually listened and responded, what would they do with the notion of invoking the voice of an unborn and perhaps never-to-exist great grand-daughter?

BTW, XR appears to have appropriated this song from the peoplesclimate.org group, substantially degrading it by putting a negative spin on what in the original has a much more positive vibe; see https://youtu.be/QKcA9LS8Qpc for the original.

Thanks as always for this stimulating and informative forum!

replies.title: Re: Do chants evoke magical entities?
replies.content: No, you might as well chant "Eeny meenie miney moe" and expect the spirits to answer. It's typically lame radical street theater with bad poetry to match. I bet they succeed in summoning a powerful spirit of self-righteous pomposity in themselves, but that's about it.

replies.title: Re: Do chants evoke magical entities?
replies.content: First impression from as Wiccan PoV: They're marching widdershins (counter-clockwise)?  You go widdershins to banish something, so if they do succeed in raising energy (not overly likely), it will be working in opposition to the statement of intent here.

comments.title: Family protection
comments.content:
I’m afraid that someone is taking advantage from my parents. She comes from my mother’s home country and used to take care of my grandparents while they were alive. During that time, she rented an apartment in the same house and is still living there, although it’s too expensive for her. She’s on unemployment benefits and keeps refusing jobs. Seems to spend too much in other areas of life as well (Oh, I just got my check and it all went into cosmetics - wasn’t that much anyway!), while my mother is helping out with money and gifts.

1. How can I find out if my family is in danger? Geomancy with multiple significators?

2. If the answer is yes, how to proceed without telling my parents? Could a bottle/river charm separate me from them as well? Would a witch bottle need their consent?

(For what it’s worth, Folk Magic is very common in my mother’s country, so the person in question might recognize it; she has made hints about itafew times.)

replies.title: Re: Family protection
replies.content:
First of all, have you talked to your parents about this? They may be perfectly well aware of the situation, and may be making their own choice in the matter, which is of course their right. You can certainly do geomancy to scope out the situation, but don't use multiple significators unless you're already experienced enough to do that effectively. Before you do anything, you need your parents' consent -- that's a hard and fast rule.

(Have you by any chance read Proust's Remembrance of Things Past? There's a great scene in the first volume, "Swann's Way," where the narrator's Aunt Leonie is tne center of just such a situation: her servant Josephine and her friend Eulalie are each utterly certain that the other is taking advantage of Leonie, and things spiral out into subtle strangeness from there...)

replies.title: Re: Family protection
replies.content:
Dear Archdruid, thank you for your answer. I have talked to them, and so did another old friend of theirs. They were skeptical about it, and of course would never approve of any measure taken. It is of course their choice. I was just startled by some strange behaviors of that person, like giving me something in front of my parents, and taking it away under some pretext when they weren’t there. Or telling them something that made my mother stop talking to me for a few days, or always interrupting when I start talking to them about something. I’m trying to watch out if I’m doing anything to cause those situations without noticing it - I’m aware that she knows how I feel and might feel the same way. But I would like to know if she can really be trusted.

I’m really worried about what happens if one of my parents dies or gets sick. I feel like I must take care of them, and don’t want to be stuck with this woman who refused to help when my grandmother was dying while my parents were abroad and couldn’t see it.

Could I do a working for my own protection and which kind would be safe and without harm to my relation with my family?

I’ve practiced Geomancy for a while, so would like to try a reading - fourth house and seventh?

comments.title: Etheric Effect of Breastfeeding
comments.content:
You've mentioned food (particularly meat) having an etheric affect, and I'm curious whether a child's readiness to wean from breastmilk could be related to this. I have friends whose children self-weaned as young as 9 months (though most much later). Meanwhile, I am struggling to wean my now-3-year-old Fooftawoo Banisher. She shows no interest whatsoever in self-weaning (she would nurse 4-6 times a day if I let her) and I want her to be done nursing before the pending arrival of Baby #2. I have moved gradually, adding more and more limits, and we are now down to 1 very short (less than 1 minute) nursing session a day. My hubby says it's time to cut it off, but I am hesitant without being able to articulate why. I am not personally attached to nursing. Clearly she doesnt "need" to nurse for nutrition, and she gets plenty of cuddles. Yet somehow she still seems to "need" it. It affects her entire demeanor.
As I type this, I'm realizing that she has started getting scared at night since we've reduced to 1 nursing session daily. I wonder if it's coincidence or connected. And if connected, whether the source of that is just emotional or if there are other elements.
Rather than fooftawoos, she says she's scared of "the bees," and has even run to my bed once in the wee hours complaining about bees in her room. Mind you, we have never ever seen a bee in our apartment, our windows are screened, and our building manager has an exterminator come around routinely for maintenance so I almost never see bugs of any kind in all the years we've lived here. Also she has not had negative encounters with bees outside- I like to point them out to her and calmly tell her to give them space while we watch them pollinating flowers. So this sudden fear of the "bees in her room" is quite confusing. I'm navigating how to help her with her bees, and prayer seems to do okay. You mentioned bay laurel being protective so I may get that oil soon to diffuse in her room in addition. I'm open to other suggestions. (I don't practice ritual magic, btw.)
Anyway, are there theories out there about sensitive children and their nursing habits? (She does seem to still have some degree of clairvoyance. She knew I was pregnant before I did. She also knew that the baby was a boy- and was distinctly unhappy about that fact- a week before the ultrasound technician confirmed it.) Obviously she has to wean some time, and I want that time to be now... but not to her detriment if she "needs" it. In case it's relevant, she currently refuses to eat any meat except for chicken nuggets- and that only 2 or 3 times in a week. I do serve her various meats regularly but she has no interest. Her protein comes from peanut butter, greek yogurt, and cheese. And maybe twice a month she'll deign to eat an egg. But if I understand correctly, the need for meat has more to do with grounding after magical practice, rather than clairvoyance... or am I mistaken? (Apologies, I am only an occasional reader here, so I can definitely be mis-remembering.)

Thank you for this space to ask questions.
Sincerely,
Mom of the Fooftawoo Banisher

replies.title: Re: Etheric Effect of Breastfeeding
replies.content:
My experience as someone with fairly powerful psychic intuition is that meat reduces it greatly. It's entirely possible that she's unhappy with it: imagine if eating something blurred your vision, and that's about what meat does for me, and likely for your daughter as well.

There are times when I find it really helpful, indeed there are times I'll go out of my way to get a burger, but a little kid probably wouldn't be able to figure it out.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Effect of Breastfeeding
replies.content:
Thank you for the insight. It's good that there is no hindrance to weaning. The new baby hasnt arrived yet - isn't due until next year. So far she has been very loving and excited. She rubs my tummy and talks to the new baby. She tells him stories, sings him songs, and frequently asks me if the baby is dancing, or stretching, or resting. She talks to and about the new baby, and makes sure to include him in good night rituals and other things. Now that doesnt mean the jealousy won't rear its head after the little guy is here and actually taking away so much attention, but she has so far been delighted to be a big sister.
But that doesnt mean there havent already been big changes, apart from the weaning. My father was killed in a car crash this summer, when a driver going the opposite direction crossed the median and hit him head-on. Also, I've experienced terrible morning sickness for the majority of this pregnancy, which greatly altered our routine and hindered my ability to parent like I want. My girl has weathered this upheaval admirably. She is so kind; standing next to me and rubbing my back when I'm vomiting, and contenting herself with sit-down activities like coloring and Legos for the vast majority of the day when I cannot manage anything else. I am beyond grateful for how considerate and seemingly unruffled she is from it all- and am doing everything I can to keep it together on my own part- that I havent considered how the bees and the resulting need for extra comfort could very well be due to all these changes that otherwise havent seemed to affect her much.

replies.title: Re: Etheric Effect of Breastfeeding
replies.content: Hmm. Is it possible that ordinary sibling jealousy is also involved? It's far from rare for an older child to feel shoved out of the way by the arrival of a new baby, and the ways the older child tries to retain a special status with Mom are many and varied. Meat will certainly close down the psychic senses a bit, and that can be a good thing if a child needs to come to terms with the material world; I don't know of any reason she should still actually need to nurse, so you can certainly go ahead and wean her now. Learning that you can't always get what you want is an essential lesson of life...

replies.title: Re: breastfeeding ans sibling rivalry
replies.content:
I still recall the look of utter dismay my son (about 18 months old) gave me when he saw his baby sister nursing for the first time. I continued to let him nurse for naps and bedtime for several months. Then I decided to wean him over his protests because I was just exhausted from lack of sleep. I was deep into La Leche League, co-sleeping, child led weaning, etc. at the time. But something had to give.

Interestingly I was still nursing my oldest when I became pregnant the second time (with my son). I had not planned to wean because of the pregnancy, but I developed a physical aversion to the act. This did not occur in pregnancy #3, I have no idea why, although it may have been because 2 and 3 were closer together than 1 and 2 (28 months apart). I think I read somewhere that the mother and child share aura (etheric body?) until weaning.

replies.title: Re: bees in the bedroom
replies.content: I've been following your accounts of your daughter since you began posting them. I think that she is calling these beings or phenomena bees because that is the nearest word she has in her vocabulary to what they are like. You have determined that they are not physical insects, so what are they? Perhaps it would help if you asked your daughter some questions about the bees. For example: Are they spirit bees? What are they doing? Are bees in the room all the time or only sometimes? How does she know when they are there? How are the bees bothering her?

replies.title: Re: Etheric Effect of Breastfeeding
replies.content:
It's extremely common for the eldest child to feel hatred and rage--these are strong terms, I know, but realistic ones fror many a child--toward the second child when it comes into being, and to cling by any convenient means to only-child status. A second child actually is an almost existential threat to the oldest child's previous view of herself and her place in the world--not to her actual self and place in the world, but to her perception of these things. (Parents are usually too excited about the new baby to even notice how their older child's reaction differs from their own.)

At the same time, by age 3, the child probably knows that expressing and acting on such strong feelings is unacceptable. So much of an eldest child's actual strategies to deal with the changing situation of her life will take place below the level of her consciousness.. "The bees" may have been formed--unconsciously, I would emphasize--as a danger to justify her demands for extra comfort in her time of genuine perceived need.

If her "bees" were much the same sort of beings as the fooftawoos that she perceives in other contexts, she would probably also call them fooftawoos. Since she doesn't, they are probably something else.

One possibility is that she has called these "bees" into existence herself. If so, you could ask her to devise a very simple protective ritual (with your help, of course) against them. Another possibility is that they exist independently of her. There, too, a self-devised protective ritual may help a lot, though the actual devising is a bit trickier, needing more trial-and-error testing. Treat it something like an art project with real-world consequences.

Either way, give her the agency to figure out for herself how she can guard herself against the changes she now must face in her life. From what you've already written about her, I expect that she will be more than equal to the challenge. The more of her, and the less of you (her parents), she puts into that ritual of hers, the more power it will gove her to work through this enormous change taking place in her life (as she has known it up to now).

replies.title: Re: Etheric Effect of Breastfeeding
replies.content:
Writing as a mom of six, she may not physically need to nurse but she may very well emotionally need to be close to you. I was lucky in that my milk changed and the older child found it distasteful very early in pregnancy, before there was any talk of new baby.

She's losing her relationship as your baby (and gaining one as your big girl, but she cannot see a gain) and the new baby is getting one. Big siblings can be bitterly jealous. If not nursing, which is a drain on you, what can be specifically kept that's part of your and her relationship? Fixing her hair in the morning? Pick something and do your best to keep it going even in those first days when everything falls apart, even if that means, hey, it's four pm, but we're finally getting to our morning tea or whatever you pick.

Or can you put into place some special big girl ritual that babies don't get to do? Not magic, but something like "girls night" when you and she go do something just for you, with the explicit understanding that Daddy will keep the baby? Can you reassure her that new babies aren't any fun: they can't do anything, they need so much, and of course we love them, but they're just babies? How much can she as a big girl help get things ready for that poor helpless baby?

And I now hear my children revolting, someone is teasing the eight-year-old from the screams, and screaming at her big brothers for teasing her is one of her favorite pastimes . . . right up until I kick them all outside to scream to their hearts' delights!

BoysMom

comments.title: Hermetic Third Eye?

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Third Eye?
replies.content: Plato, in a lost esoteric work of his, wrote of the third eye in humans; but all of his esoteric works have been lost over the centuries, so we have no way of knowing just what he had to say about it.  (His extant dialogues were all written as exoteric works, to be freely circulated.)  Our only source for the bare fact of that Plato mentioned the "third eye" comes to us at third hand, where the Christian writer Origen is trying to refute an earlier Pagan critic of Christianity, Celsus by name.  He quotes snippets of Celsus's book to refute them.  It is one of these snippets from Celsus's lost work that mentions Plato and the "third eye."

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Third Eye?
replies.content: Hmm! I clearly need to read Origen more closely.

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Third Eye?
replies.content:
The most interesting of Origen's works, for an esotericiost, is his "Contra Celsum," an attempted--not always successful--refutation of Celsus's lost treatise against Christianity (titled Logos Alêthês = "True Word"). Celsus is early enough (2nd century) that he had access to lots of primary written and oral sources now lost, and his take on Jesus is that he was one of the wandering magicians of his era, and a quite skilled and successful one, at that. (This seems to have been the majority take on Jesus among non-Christians, wbhether Jews or Pagans, from the 2nd century down through the centuries. See in this connection Morton Smith's fine piece of quite readable scholarship, "Jesus the Magician," 1978.)

The best scholarly translation into English of Origen's work seems tio be Henry Chadwick's.

replies.title: Re: Hermetic Third Eye?
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. If you can find a copy of Manly P. Hall's Man, the Grand Symbol of the Mysteries, you'll find an extensive discussion of the pituitary and pineal centers in the brain.

replies.title: Re: Psychic centres
replies.content: Okay, we're getting into fairly complex territory here. There are 360 psychic centers in the human body, and they each have different functions. The ones along the midline of the body are the most important. Different systems of spiritual and magical practice focus on different centers -- for example, the seven chakras are seven out of the 33 centers along the spine; the Middle Pillar uses four of the spinal centers and the center at the soles of the feet; the Grail working in The Druid Magic Handbook uses three centers along the midline, and so on. Different systems choose the centers they want to use based on what specific abilities their practices are meant to develop. So a lot depends on what exactly she intends you to do, and you're more likely to find that out from her than from me...

comments.title: Newbie questions
comments.content:
Hi JMG

a newbie here. I have a few questions and comments about my magical training.

1) The Sphere of Protection. It is going well; I can feel the energy flowing through me. But during the closing phase, I don't really know what to do with my arms. I'm supposed to cross them on my chest at the end, but before that? I found that touching the solar plexus with my thumbs helps me focus on the initial establishment of the sphere.

2) Meditation. All and all its going OK, my body can quickly relax and I feel the warmth circling through me without losing the attention focus. I do a 15- or 20-minute meditation daily, depending on the topic. The problems I have are mostly external, I'm currently living in a very small house along with my relatives and there is little privacy. I don't let them know about my eccentric hobby, so I have to improvise e.g. the SOP I do in the bathroom. With meditation I can't take a proper posture without questions being asked, so I pretend to nap sitting on the coach when I meditate. I know it may not be optimal, but I think it’s important to practice now rather than wait for conditions to improve.

3) Divination. I use Ogham (in my case cards) to cast divination. The results are mixed. Sometimes the reading is spot on, other times its vague and hard to decipher. The problem I have is that I don't know what to ask. There are few burning questions, certainly not enough to do a divination every day. Can I just ask "What can I expect from the coming day?". Then again, I'm a little reluctant because I feel that if I do a divination I will be bound by its results, if that makes any sense.

Thank you for hosting Magic Mondays. May the Druid gods bless you.

P.S. Why did you feature Merlin and Bleys? I am unaware!

replies.title: Re: Newbie questions
replies.content:
1) You can leave your arms at your sides, or place your thumbs at your solar plexur, or work in any other gestural pattern that works for you.

2) You do what you have to do. You'll need to work on your posture once you have the chance, but that can wait until the opportunity permits.

3) Yes, you can certainly ask "What can I expect from the coming day?" As for being bound by the results of the divination, remember that a divination simply shows you the pattern of events that's already there, and you're bound by that whether you cast the reading or not.

4) Heh heh heh...

comments.title: geomancy, alcohol and dying
comments.content:
Hello JMG and readers, happy MM.

This week I have questions on three topics.

Someone a year ago mentioned working with dying people who were mentally ready to go, but whose bodies seemed to be holding them back, even stealing energy to prolong physical life. I have witnessed a similar situation. This indicates that there can be a tug-of-war in different directions among the various parts of a self, and obvious as that may sound, I found it unexpected in relation to dying.
I am curious as to how trying to mitigate this might be understood in light of medical ethics and notions of suicide/euthanasia. John, do you consider finding a way out in such a situation tantamount to the chickening out that puts you back in the same classroom next time around? To what extent does that inform your opinion of euthanasia? 
I also wonder how the resources of natural magic might contribute to approaching such cases. Remembering that there are plenty of plants and substances with magical connections to death, is this a useful direction to consider putting them to use?

Secondly, a few years ago I read a library copy of the Art and Practice of Geomancy. Thinking I might wish to own it I went to an online bookseller only to be confronted by a second book, Earth Divination, Earth Magic, which was published this year! Sorry if there has been discussion of it on this site - I couldn't find any - so could you tell me, John, if it covers the same practical material as the first? If not, could you give a brief outline of the respective focii of the two?

Lastly, regarding recipes for magical ink, herbal macerations, Florida water and the like which call for high proof alcohol. In Australia we have difficulty sourcing such a thing. Our caring legislators have ensured that it is nigh impossible to buy spirits with over 40% ethanol content. Those closer to 50% are things like gin, which already contain herbal extracts and so are likely to be unsuitable. In the scientific arena, much more pure alcohol is used for sterilising. But the supply of such chemicals is restricted. What we here call methylated spirits, aka denatured alcohol, is not recommended for magical use. 
Can rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol) be substituted for ethanol in such recipes?
Have Australian readers found a route to success despite the overprotective environs?

Thanks all,

kallianeira.

replies.title: Re: geomancy, alcohol and dying
replies.content:
1) It's hard to hand down any kind of general rule; prayer is probably your best bet in such cases, because the gods know the details and you don't.

2) Earth Divination, Earth Magic was my first book on geomancy, and it was just reprinted this year; The Art and Practice of Geomancy was published after the original publisher dropped my earlier book. They cover essentially the same material when it comes to geomantic divination, though their material on geomantic magic differs -- the earlier book used Golden Dawn-style magic, the latter book Renaissance astrological magic.

3) Yes, you can use rubbing alcohol for anything you won't be taking into your body. For something you'll be drinking, use good brandy. I suspect it's quite illegal for you to take good brandy, pour it into a pan, put the pan in the freezer, and strain out the ice that forms -- that's an effective way of concentrating the alcohol -- so by all means be careful not to do that.

;-)

replies.title: Re: geomancy, alcohol and dying
replies.content:
Ya know, I had decided on more discretion hence wasn't going to mention distillation as a possibility... but that freezing trick, if I'd known it the question would not even have had to be asked!

Thanks!

k

replies.title: Re: geomancy, alcohol and dying
replies.content:
That's the way that people in northern Europe used to make hard liquor back before distillation got there from China, where it was invented. They'd take a vat of apple cider that's been fermented good and hard, and leave it out night after night, scooping out the ice each morning. Of course if it's illegal where you are you should of course never dream of doing the same thing with a wide shallow pan and your freezer.

;-)

replies.content:

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
One more geomancy question please. You know some of the history of our primitive off-grid hippie palace that we built and lived in for 7 years before moving to town into a house that we still have to buy (from my grandmother).

We have two primary ideas about what to do with the old homestead: 1) sell it, while the market still has a pulse, pay half of the new house off and use the leftovers for energy descent retrofitting (reducing monthly bills in the process), and 2) rent it on Airbnb (or similar), expecting it to do well because of what it is even if the market goes south, and still have it for one of our children a decade from now if they want to move back out there. That option leaves us without an easy, or potentially very large, down payment, and lots of new house left to pay for. Maybe.

Attempting to divine the right course of action I've cast two shield charts concerning the options. The first question was "Would selling (our homestead) be a long-term benefit to my immediate family?" I worked out Rubeus as the Right Witness, Puella as the Left, and Via as the Judge.

The second question was "Would keeping (our homestead) be a long-term benefit to my immediate family?" This time I got Puer as the Right Witness, Cauda Draconis as the Left, and Fortuna Major as the Judge.

The way I read this is that, in the first question, Rubeus is the querent, me, dangerously passionate about the homestead and what it means to me philosophically and sentimentally. Puella sits in for the homestead itself, as a stable, harmonious, feminine (I've always thought of the place as a she), Libra type, though fickle and ambivalent, requiring relationship and interaction to be complete. I'm now unable to give that to "her" as much as I feel I need to, and know that parting ways would be very difficult for me, but seeing other people living there, maybe mistreating her, would be worse. Via as Judge seems like it's screaming "big change!" but doesn't seem to point to an answer one way or the other.

In the second, I see myself as Puer, the petulant boy, either wanting to keep my ball to play with or get it sold so I can improve (and buy more easily) my new one. Cauda draconis is favorable for endings, but given the question and the Judge Fortuna Major, I have to wonder if it isn't pointing me to letting go of the idea of selling her, getting settled into the idea of making money with her and not living there anymore, and keeping her for the long haul, maybe for my son who loves the place dearly, to maybe move into later (or keep making money with if not). Save all that Airbnb money and buy the new place with it. If we could put away 15k from weekend rentals every year for the next decade the new place would be practically paid for, and we'd still have the old homestead too.

From an agricultural perspective, it's like choosing whether our cow is for beef or for milk.

Given the background, does this seem like a reasonable interpretation of these figures to you? I realize two witnesses and a Judge, from my first two chart castings ever, isn't a lot to go on...

Again, many thanks!
Tripp

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Pretty much. In questions like this I tend to read right witness as the past, judge as the present, and left witness as the future. Selling the house will be challenging but have a better outcome in the long term. Renting it out will be considerably more lucrative for the short term but it's not a strategy with a long shelf life.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Questions like what? Questions specifically about long-term benefits?

Interesting plot twists on my initial read. So when you talk about a short shelf life do you think we might get the 10 years of rental income I mentioned before passing it on to our son? That kind of short-term? Or is your take that the shelf life in question is considerably shorter than that? That perhaps the overall message that matters is to sell the house while the selling's good and invest in our in-town property instead? Or that I shouldn't put too much faith in my first two chart castings?? :)

Tripp

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: No, questions specifically about practical matters where there's a time factor. As for how much time, you'd have to cast further divinations to find that out.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
This is probably my prepper side talking here, but what concerns me most about your two interpretations is that they could both be pointing to a coming market bust, in which case we might not be able to sell it or rent it. But given your recent mundane astrology posts I'm inclined to believe that's an overly pessimistic view. Especially in an area that's seen wild population growth since being voted the 2nd nicest town in America, with no shortage of rising-sea-level refugees all along the southeastern coast to keep feeding the trend.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Okay, as I noted in response to this Magic Monday's first question, if you're going to practice geomancy, start with the basic interpretation. Since you're doing a house chart, you look at your significator (in the first house) and the significator of the quesited (in the ninth house, since it's a question about religion). The figure in the ninth house, Populus, passes to the twelfth into conjunction with Laetitia in the first, which means that the answer to your question is "Yes." When last week you mentioned it was demonic, this linked with another piece of occult lore I've come across: you never want to work with demons because it'll strengthen the demon and their ability to influence you.

replies.content:
Here's my argument: Pluto correlates strongly with death and wealth. Fossil fuels provide an enormous amount of wealth, and are from dead things. This seems to suggest a connection. Additionally, Hades in Greek mythology is a feared figure, although he has his positives: he is the only god never described as having an affair (loyalty); he is also brutal, but fair and just. These elements are missing from Pluto.

The planet Pluto behaves oddly in general: each of the planets seems to manifest their way of existing from the heights of human capacity down to the demonic. Pluto, however, seems to be lacking most of that, and seems to manifest almost exclusively in negative, destructive ways.

The other interesting element is that Plutonic cultural themes started taking shape during the late 1700s/early 1800s in Western Europe/North America, in particular in the form of the powerful obsession over wealth and power, and the toxic dream of the conquest over nature.

Finally, an enormous number of Plutonic themes, from pesticides and antibiotics, through to LSD (synthetic drugs, underworld tied), chemical weapons, nuclear power, TV, the consumer economy, global dimming, and on and on rely on fossil fuels.

In particular, they rely on oil. The oil age got started in the first part of the 1900s and thus looks very Plutonic, but even with Pluto fading out doesn't look like it'll end by 2036. This suggests the demon in question is still around, and if Pluto did rule oil, it makes sense to assume it is the same entity.

Thoughts?

replies.content: I consider it essential to do some kind of protective practice before discursive meditation. A religious person can use prayer and scripture study for that purpose; otherwise, I'd encourage you to learn how to teach the Sphere of Protection -- that's a simple ritual that almost anyone can learn and practice -- and pass that onto people along with discursive meditation.

replies.title: Re: safeguards for discursive meditation?

replies.title: Re: safeguards for discursive meditation?

replies.title: Re: Planetary colors

The classic set of colors is this:

Sun/Sunday: Yellow
Moon/Monday: Violet
Mars/Tuesday: Red
MercuryWednesday: Orange
Jupiter/Thursday: Blue
Venus/Friday: Green
Saturn/Saturday: Black

comments.title: Combining yoga and western esoterosism
comments.content:
Good morning JMG and all! As per our comments last week on your project combining yoga with banishing rituals,etc, I put some thoughts together and will try to keep it brief.

I guess I started shifting the energy around when I read a discussion in one of JMG’s very own blogs about the tendency of yogis to become egomaniac blowhards due to all the solar energy concentrated in the heart center. Jinkies! It’s true. Slowly slowly in the classes I teach we began moving our focus down to the four corners of the feet to find our place of balance. Trying to diffuse all that energy a little bit.

Then I stripped back all the energy breath work to good old fashioned deep, controlled abdominal breathing, with some light ujjayi pranayama for ashtanga practice.

The only energy work I kept was bandha practice (energy locks) because the physical benefits alone are profound but it is primarily an energetic practice.

So the time had come to pass to make some really fundamental changes in my life and I needed something more than yoga to give me that push to make it happen. Also I really needed a banishing ritual for protection again a lot of really hateful vibes from all quarters. When people change, other people don’t like their boat rocked...the lonely path indeed! Besides, your wonderful commenters we’re having such a great time I wanted to join in.

Following JMG’s instructions to the letter, over the course of several months I have begun the polytheistic LBRP (Greek pantheon), the MP, reciting the Orphic hymn to the planet of the day (not memorized yet!) and discursive meditation. The immediate protection from my patron goddess has been amazing and deeply comforting. Moving energy in the middle pillar has been pretty easy and delightful. It is very clear where any little “snags” are located in the energy body. Discursive meditation is coming along. It was easier than I thought it would be to mediate in a sitting position rather than twisted up like a pretzel :) but I never did get into “mindless” meditation despite it’s being fashionable.

Also, my Ishta devata for yoga was always Ganesh who has given me many blessings. Ganesh is on board with my work with Greek gods and goddesses .

So far everything has been working out very well and i’ve not noticed any weaknesses of any kind physically, energetically or otherwise. Slowly, slowly!

Lastly I would like to thank all the commenters here at magic Monday for asking all the questions everyone else wants to know too! Thanks as always JMG for hosting and being our guide. Whenever you get closer to your project on this topic, if you would ever need more details about work in the shala with real live students or something to experiment with just let me know. Namaste

PS would you still like for me to move over to the SoP? For research purposes of course :)

comments.title: Metallic correspondences and changing gods
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thanks as always for this incredible resource that you are providing.

I've saved up several questions that have occurred to me over the weeks.

1) Gold is solar, silver is lunar. To what does white gold correspond? I do have ENM, but I don't recall seeing this in there.

2) Still on the topic of metals, it seems like aluminum is a good fit for a Uranian metal. Uranus was discovered in 1781. Efforts to produce pure aluminum began in 1760, succeeded in producing small quantities by 1824, and yielded an effective industrial process by 1886. Aluminum is almost never found as a native metal, and requires technology to produce in any quantity. It's association with modernity and with consumer items would seem to fit Uranian themes of disruption and individuation. How would one know? Meditation? Trying a natural magic style amulet using aluminum?

3) You've talked about how gods and goddesses can change their areas of responsibility over time. Is this what has happened with Hecate since ancient times? Hesiod, who devoted a significant paragraph to her, indicates that she is very powerful and well-regarded by all (mentioned are: kings, soldiers, athletes, horsemen, fishermen, shepherds, and nursing mothers).

Excerpt: "And she conceived and bare Hecate whom Zeus the son of Cronos honoured above all. He gave her splendid gifts, to have a share of the earth and the unfruitful sea. She received honour also in starry heaven, and is honoured exceedingly by the deathless gods. For to this day, whenever any one of men on earth offers rich sacrifices and prays for favour according to custom, he calls upon Hecate. Great honour comes full easily to him whose prayers the goddess receives favourably, and she bestows wealth upon him; for the power surely is with her...."

There is also the passage in (I think) the Homeric Hymmns where she helps Demeter search for Persephone after her abduction by Hades. There is certainly an association with darkness here, but much more as a helpful guide through it, than as a bringer of it.

These days, it seems that only her darker, witchier, (crone) aspects are emphasized, and that she is no longer a goddess of the people (maiden and mother) in general. On the other hand, I have seen some discussion other places online where there are people interacting with her more in the ancient way. What are your thoughts on Hecate?

Regards,

Steve

replies.title: Re: Metallic correspondences and changing gods
replies.content:
1) Alloys -- and white gold is an alloy of gold with some white metal, usually nickel or silver -- have mixed rulerships and therefore generally can't be used in magic.

2) Meditation followed by experimental magical workings would be the usual way to do it.

3) The Hellenic pantheon isn't one I've worked with extensively, and the only deities from that pantheon I have any experience with are the planetary deities. Thus I'm not at all sure what to say. You might want to talk to some Hellenic polytheists and see what they have to say.

comments.title: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

1) In last week's Magic Monday you mentioned that you know of a few magical casualties. Could you tell us what happened, and more importantly, what caused these people to become casualties and how we can avoid ending up in a similar situation. I know that in chi kung there is an idea of "chi sickness", damage to the energetic system that occurs due to wrong or too much practice resulting in physical and/or mental illness. Is it something similar or more drastic (e.g., being eaten by stray shoggoth)?

2) You also mentioned last week that you would avoid any assumption of godforms in magical work. Why is this? And how can one avoid getting into trouble with this if one is already in a tradition that has this practice to some degree.

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content:
1) The people I know who've died as a result of bad magical practice got deep into noxious magic of various kinds, and then had sudden, aggressive illnesses pop up out of nowhere and kill them in a fairly short time. To avoid that, a good grasp of magical ethics is essential, and it's also very important to follow instructions exactly when you're doing any energy work that awakens centers inside the body; those are safe if practiced as instructed, but aren't always safe under other conditions.

2) Godform work is risky because it can lead to a highly inflated ego. You can stay relatively safe by praying and burning incense to the deity and asking for their help in remmebering who's the god and who's not!

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content:
What exactly is noxious magic? Is it magic aimed at harming/cursing others or is it working with demons, Necronomicon entities, Goetia etc?

Thanks again!

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content: The simple answer is that cacomagic, to give what I've called "noxious magic" its proper name, is magic that's done with no attention to its impact on the wider world. I'd encourage you to meditate on that...

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content: Is Godform work the same as "drawing down the moon"?

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content: No, drawing down is simple possession work, the sort of thing mediums do. Assuming a godform involves creating an astral image of a deity and taking it on yourself as an interface for working with the deity's energy; it's wholly conscious and wholly deliberate, and does not involve trance.

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content: What is the purpose of same,

replies.title: Re: Magical casualties & not assuming godforms
replies.content: Manifesting those energies in initiatory work. You'll find a detailed discussion in Book 3 of The Golden Dawn by Israel Regardie.

That the ancient Druids practiced human sacrifice is pretty much beyond question, as it is born out by both classical accounts and archaeology. We have talked here about how similar practices in other civilizations brought down catastrophe-- the Aztecs under the Spanish and European Christendom in the century following the Inquisition are the examples that come to mind. Druidry as a river. Over time the river became polluted with human bodies, and so it withdrew from this world-- into the Underground, where it could be purified, in the same way that water is purified by passing through rocks and sand. While it was underground it functioned as an aquifer, from which water could be drawn in wells; and this water was the enduring image of the Druid over time, and the endurance of Druidic themes in, e.g., Celtic Christianity and Welsh and Irish literature. Now the river of Druidry is re-emerging into the world. On its bank grows a great oak tree; the tree is the river, solidified, for a moment; it will die too in its time.

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Open Questions and Closed Questions in Geomancy
replies.content:
Since you've addressed this to "and all", I'll add a few thoughts to your questions on geomancy, based on my experience.

For all queries, but especially for more open-ended ones, I've often found it helpful to focus on figures which repeat in the chart. In particular, and of decreasing importance:

(1) Do the figures of the Court show up anywhere else earlier in the chart? If so, there's often something very important there. (I know this goes against JMG's advice to ignore the court in a house chart. Nonetheless, it's what works for me. Frankly, at this point I see the 12 houses when I look at the shield chart—it is, after all, the very same information, just presented in a different shape—and I know of other geomancers who seem to do likewise.)

(2) Do other important signifiers pass elsewhere in the chart? In your examples, these would be the figures in the first and seventh houses.

(3) Are there any other repeated figures?

Again, for open-ended queries, the Way of Points is often your friend. Try it with any of the four elements in the Judge which are active; don't limit yourself to just the Fire element.


Finally, to whether closed questions are "better": in my experience, they're certainly *easier*, especially given how literal-minded the Spirits of this particular oracle are, but they'll answer both types. It just takes a good deal more work to understand the answer in the more complicated, "open" cases.

replies.title: Re: Two questions
replies.content:
1) That's part of the historical cycle, as Vico shows. Words start out meaty and concrete, and become vaporous and abstract as society moves from the barbarism of sense at its beginning to the barbarism of reflection at its end.

comments.title: Meditation Themes
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Could you give a few pointers about selecting themes for meditation from a larger work? For example, if I was going to work my way through the Mabinogion bit by bit, would you recommend using each and every line or phrase as a meditation theme?

replies.title: Re: Meditation Themes
replies.content:
The basic rule is to make every individual idea its own theme. If you have a personal or place name, try to find out what it means -- in the Mabinogion, none of the names are there by chance! The smaller the piece, the deeper you can go with it -- and there are no low-content sentences in the Mabinogion, just sentences you don't know how to unpack yet.

The rule for meditating on the Bible, as I was taught it, is to begin with the Gospel of John and spend at least one session on every single verse. Then go on to the other Gospels, Acts, the epistles, and Revelation; then the psalms; in all cases, one verse at a time at most. Many people just focus on those. The long genealogies have an enormous amount to teach if you know Hebrew and have a basic grasp of the Cabalistic methods of unpacking meaning from names, but not everyone learns those.

comments.title: The truth hurts, sometimes ......

replies.title: Re: The truth hurts, sometimes ......
replies.content: The real history of occultism is far more interesting and colorful than the fake history that's been circulated about it. What's more, I've found consistently that the people who are interested in the real history of the Druid Revival, say, tend to be better students, more willing to put in the work needed to learn and practice the teachings, than those who are attracted by bogus claims about a lineage going straight back to the ancient Druids. So to my mind, it's high time to ditch the grandmother stories and Secret Chiefs and conveniently untraceable lineages going back to trilobites in the Upper Devonian, and talk about the actual history of our tradition, in all its richness, drama, spiritual significance, and occasional bouts of absurdity. We'll end up with a better tradition.
